    • Why One HTML Engine Matters
  • Design

Why One HTML Engine Matters

Source: vignettes/single-engine.Rmd
single-engine.Rmd

You have a site. You want to add R.

You already have a blog built with Hugo, Jekyll, Eleventy, or another static site generator. You have a theme you like, a build pipeline that works, and shortcodes you’ve written. Now you want to include some R output — a plot, a table, maybe an interactive widget — in a post.

The standard advice is to reach for blogdown or Quarto. Both work, but both change your relationship with your site generator.

The two-engine problem

blogdown routes your .Rmd through Pandoc before Hugo sees it. Quarto does the same. The result is two independent Markdown-to-HTML engines in series:

.Rmd / .qmd
  → knitr (evaluates R)
    → Pandoc (converts Markdown to HTML)
      → Hugo / Jekyll (converts Markdown to HTML again)
        → final site

Pandoc and your SSG are both Markdown-to-HTML converters, but they are different converters with different rules. Each has its own opinions about CommonMark extensions, raw HTML handling, and template features. This creates a practical problem: you write for two parsers at once.

Shortcodes break

Hugo shortcodes like {{</* figure src="plot.svg" */>}} must survive Pandoc processing untouched. This means guarding them — wrapping them in raw HTML blocks, using special escape syntax, or writing separate shortcode versions for Pandoc-processed content and plain Markdown content.

This isn’t a one-time setup cost. Every shortcode you write or adopt has to account for whether the content passed through Pandoc first. Authors working on the Hugo side must think about Pandoc escaping, which is unnatural for someone working in Hugo’s template language.

Two Markdown dialects in one site

With blogdown or Quarto, .Rmd / .qmd files go through Pandoc and your SSG. Plain .md files go through your SSG only. The two paths produce subtly different HTML from similar-looking Markdown. CSS and JS from Pandoc’s template system can conflict with the SSG theme.

The result is context-switching: Pandoc-flavoured Markdown in R files, SSG-flavoured Markdown in everything else. Similar enough to look interchangeable, different enough to break.

statdown: drop Pandoc, keep your SSG

statdown takes the opposite approach from Quarto. Instead of pulling the website into R’s toolchain, it pushes R output into your SSG’s world:

.Rmd
  → knitr (evaluates R, splices results into Markdown)
    → CommonMark .md (with raw HTML blocks for widgets)
      → Hugo / Jekyll / any SSG (the only HTML engine)
        → final site

knitr is not an HTML engine. It evaluates R code chunks and inserts the results — text, tables, plot references, widget HTML — into the Markdown source. It does not parse or transform the surrounding Markdown. The output is plain CommonMark with optional raw HTML blocks.

One system converts Markdown to HTML: your static site generator. You write for one parser, one set of rules, one mental model.

What this looks like in practice

Shortcodes just work

Because statdown outputs CommonMark, SSG features pass through knitr unchanged. Hugo shortcodes, Jekyll includes, and Eleventy shortcodes are just text in the Markdown source — no escaping, no guarding, no special cases.

---
title: "My Post"
---

Here is a plot generated by R:

```{r}
plot(cars)
```

And here is a Hugo shortcode, used normally:

{{</* youtube dQw4w9WgXcQ */>}}

After statdown_render(), the .md output contains the plot reference and the shortcode verbatim. Hugo processes both. No Pandoc in between to mangle the shortcode syntax.

htmlwidgets

When knitr encounters an htmlwidget (e.g. a DT datatable or a leaflet map), statdown’s custom knit_print method:

  1. Extracts the widget’s CSS/JS dependencies
  2. Registers them with depkit, which deduplicates and copies assets
  3. Emits the widget’s HTML inline

After knitr finishes, statdown_render() prepends <link> tags and appends <script> tags to the .md file. These are raw HTML blocks in CommonMark — every compliant parser passes them through to the final page unchanged.

By default, JS assets are served from a CDN (jsDelivr) with SRI integrity hashes and automatic local fallback. This reduces hosting burden without sacrificing reliability.

No special cases

With one engine, there is no Pandoc Markdown vs. Hugo Markdown distinction. Your entire site uses the same Markdown dialect: whatever your SSG implements. R posts and plain posts are processed identically.

When Quarto is the better choice

Quarto solves the two-engine problem differently: it eliminates the SSG. Quarto becomes the site builder, so there is only one engine — but it’s Pandoc, not Hugo. This is a valid approach when R is the center of your site rather than an occasional guest.

Quarto also provides capabilities that statdown does not:

  • Citations and crossrefs — Pandoc’s citation processor and cross-reference system are mature and powerful.
  • Multi-format output — one source document can produce HTML, PDF, slides, and Word. statdown only produces CommonMark .md.
  • Quarto extensions — a growing ecosystem of filters, formats, and shortcodes that operate at the Pandoc layer.
  • Integrated publishing — quarto publish handles deployment to GitHub Pages, Netlify, and other hosts.

If your workflow involves academic writing, multi-format delivery, or heavy use of Pandoc features, Quarto is the better tool.

Summary

statdown is for bloggers who happen to use R — people who want to keep their static site generator and add R content without a second HTML engine in the pipeline. By outputting CommonMark and letting your SSG handle all HTML conversion, shortcodes work without workarounds, there’s one Markdown dialect across your whole site, and R is a guest in your build process rather than the host.

statdown Quarto / blogdown
Perspective blogger adds R R produces a website
HTML engines 1 (your SSG) 2 (Pandoc + SSG), or Pandoc only
Markdown dialect your SSG’s Pandoc + SSG
SSG shortcodes work directly need escaping
Widget dependencies depkit (build-time) Pandoc template + theme
Multi-format output no yes
Citations / crossrefs no yes

On this page

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
